name: SpiderFoot
description: Automated OSINT engine that queries 200+ data sources to map a target's digital footprint and attack surface.
bestFor: Pivoting from a single selector (name, email, username, IP, domain, phone) into a correlated web of related entities automatically
trustNote: 18k+ stars, long-standing flagship OSINT automation framework; SpiderFoot HX is the paid hosted version but the open-source CLI/web app is free.
notes: Run locally as a web UI or CLI. Has passive-only mode (do not touch target) vs active scanning. Many modules need free/paid API keys (Hunter, HaveIBeenPwned, Shodan, etc.) to reach full coverage. For missing persons, feed an email/username/phone and let it enumerate breach data, social accounts, and linked identifiers passively.

name: OSINT Framework
description: Web-based directory/tree of OSINT tools and resources organized by data type (email, username, social, geo, etc.).
bestFor: Discovering which specialized tool to use for a given selector type; an index rather than a single tool
trustNote: 11k+ stars, widely cited community-maintained taxonomy of OSINT tools; hosted at osintframework.com.
notes: Not a tool itself but a curated tree that routes you to the right resource per selector category. Useful as a frontier/discovery source; many leaf links are external third-party tools. Hosted version is osintframework.com.

name: TIDoS-Framework
description: Offensive web application penetration testing and reconnaissance framework with OSINT and footprinting modules.
bestFor: Web-app recon and active enumeration of a target domain/host
trustNote: 1.9k stars; offensive pentest framework, primarily infrastructure-focused.
notes: Aimed at web application security testing, not people-finding. Active scanning leaves traces; not OPSEC-safe for passive investigations.

name: vFeed
description: Correlated CVE vulnerability and threat-intelligence database with a Python API.
bestFor: Vulnerability/CVE correlation and threat intel lookups
trustNote: 949 stars; CVE/threat-intel database, free community edition with paid pro feeds.
notes: Pure cyber-threat-intelligence/vulnerability tool with no relevance to locating people.

name: ThreatIngestor
description: Extracts and aggregates threat intelligence from feeds, Twitter, RSS, and other sources into structured artifacts.
bestFor: Automated ingestion and IOC extraction from CTI feeds
trustNote: 917 stars, InQuest project for CTI pipeline automation.
notes: Could in principle scrape Twitter/RSS for a person, but designed for IOC/threat artifact extraction, not people-finding.

name: OTE (OSINT Template Engine)
description: OSINT template engine for building and running reusable reconnaissance query templates.
bestFor: Templating repeatable OSINT queries against multiple sources
trustNote: 575 stars; niche template-engine tool.
notes: Lets you define reusable query templates; situational utility for standardizing missing-persons lookups but not a data source itself.

name: server-status_PWN
description: Monitors public Apache /server-status pages to harvest URLs and request data for reconnaissance.
bestFor: Scraping exposed Apache server-status for active request intel
trustNote: 443 stars; narrow infrastructure recon tool.
notes: Infrastructure reconnaissance only; no people-finding relevance.

name: TwitWork
description: Electron/Node.js desktop app to monitor a live Twitter stream.
bestFor: Real-time monitoring of Twitter/X activity for keywords or accounts
trustNote: 272 stars; relies on the legacy Twitter API which may be deprecated/paywalled post-2023 changes.
notes: Monitors Twitter streams from a desktop Electron app. Useful for watching a person's social activity, but Twitter/X API access changes since 2023 likely break or paywall functionality. Requires Twitter API keys.

name: cURL for OSINT
description: Reference/cheat-sheet collection of cURL command recipes for OSINT data collection from web sources.
bestFor: Scriptable, low-footprint HTTP queries to OSINT endpoints from the command line
trustNote: 258 stars; curated by C3n7ral051nt4g3ncy (well-known OSINT tool author).
notes: A how-to/recipe repo rather than an installable tool; teaches using cURL to query OSINT services. Useful for automating passive lookups in agent pipelines.

name: whoishere.py
description: WiFi client detection tool that identifies people by naming devices seen issuing wireless probe requests.
bestFor: Physical-presence detection of a person via their device's WiFi probe requests
trustNote: 248 stars; requires monitor-mode WiFi hardware and physical proximity.
notes: Passively sniffs 802.11 probe requests to detect when a known device (and thus person) is physically nearby. Requires a WiFi adapter in monitor mode and on-site presence; legality varies by jurisdiction. Situational for confirming someone is at a location.

name: intelspy
description: Automated multi-threaded network reconnaissance scanner that maps and enumerates a target network.
bestFor: Active network recon and service enumeration of a host/range
trustNote: 238 stars; offensive network recon tooling.
notes: Active network scanning tool; infrastructure-focused with no people-finding use.

name: osint-tools-mcp-server
description: MCP server exposing multiple OSINT tools to AI assistants like Claude.
bestFor: Giving an LLM agent direct tool access to OSINT lookups via the Model Context Protocol
trustNote: 207 stars; directly designed for Claude/AI-assistant integration, highly relevant to an agent-driven workflow.
notes: Purpose-built to expose OSINT tools to an LLM via MCP. Highly relevant for this skill library since the consuming agent is itself Claude; can be wired in as a native MCP server to run username/email/domain lookups.

name: open-intelligence
description: Processes security camera footage with object detection, face detection, and license-plate recognition.
bestFor: Extracting faces, objects, and vehicle plates from CCTV/security-camera imagery
trustNote: 187 stars; self-hosted CCTV analysis stack.
notes: Self-hosted pipeline (TypeScript + Python ML) to run face/object/plate detection over security camera images. Relevant for analyzing surveillance footage tied to a missing person or a vehicle of interest. Requires local compute/GPU and image input.

name: Argos
description: Bootstrap script that automatically sets up a full OSINT workstation on Ubuntu.
bestFor: Provisioning a ready-to-go OSINT investigation environment with tools pre-installed
trustNote: 164 stars; install/setup automation, not a data source.
notes: Installs and configures a suite of OSINT tools on a fresh Ubuntu box. Useful for standing up an investigator environment but not itself an investigative tool; a frontier for discovering which tools it bundles.

name: DPULSE
description: Tool for a complex, comprehensive approach to domain OSINT with reporting.
bestFor: Deep domain reconnaissance and footprinting with structured reports
trustNote: 163 stars; actively maintained domain-OSINT tool.
notes: Domain-centric reconnaissance with PDF/report output. Could surface an email tied to a personal domain, but primarily infrastructure-focused.

name: XposedOrNot
description: Searches an aggregated repository of compromised/breached passwords to check exposure.
bestFor: Checking whether an email/account appears in known password breaches
trustNote: 145 stars; queries breach datasets (XposedOrNot.com API).
notes: Breach-check utility backed by the XposedOrNot dataset/API. For missing persons, confirming which breaches an email is in can reveal sites/services the person used; passive lookup, free API.

name: WhatsMyName-Python
description: Python script that checks username availability/presence across hundreds of online platforms.
bestFor: Enumerating where a given username exists across social and web platforms
trustNote: 135 stars; Python port of the well-known WhatsMyName project/wmn-data.json, by C3n7ral051nt4g3ncy.
notes: Core username-pivot tool: feed a username and it reports which of 600+ sites have an account. Directly high-value for finding a missing person's social footprint from a known handle. Passive (sends requests to each site, not to the target).

name: GitHub-Recon
description: Methodology and reference for reconnaissance and dorking against GitHub to surface leaked data.
bestFor: Finding secrets, emails, and identity leads in public GitHub repos via dorks
trustNote: 124 stars; reference/methodology repo rather than an executable tool.
notes: Documents GitHub dorking techniques. A person active on GitHub may leak their real name/email in commits; this methodology helps pivot a username/email to a real identity. Reference material, not a standalone tool.

name: shadowscatcher/shodan
description: Go client library for the Shodan.io internet-device search engine.
bestFor: Programmatic Shodan queries for exposed devices/services by IP or domain
trustNote: 119 stars; a Go SDK wrapping the Shodan API (requires Shodan API key; Shodan has free and paid tiers).
notes: Library to query Shodan from Go. Infrastructure-focused; minimal people-finding use. Requires a Shodan account/API key (free tier limited).

name: Kali Intelligence Suite (KIS)
description: Framework for fast, autonomous, centralized collection of intelligence by orchestrating standard pentest tools.
bestFor: Automating and centralizing the output of many recon/pentest tools against a target scope
trustNote: 95 stars; orchestration layer over standard Kali pentest tools.
notes: Coordinates many penetration-testing tools and stores results in a central DB. Active scanning; infrastructure-oriented, not for locating people.
